Night-Sky Monitoring
Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument
Border Corridor — VIIRS/DNB Analysis


Emilio E. Falco
June 2026

Instrument: VIIRS/DNB · VNP46A1
Source: NASA Earthdata
Period: 2020 – 2026
Southern Arizona DarkSky Chapter

The analysis uses the daily NASA Earthdata VNP46A1 atmospherically corrected product at 500 m resolution. To isolate individual sources of light change, I divided the 48 km border strip into five equal segments running W→E along the ORPI southern boundary. Monthly medians are computed from moon-free, cloud-filtered nights only.

Two time periods are shown. The first covers 2020–2022, including two major construction events in 2021. The second covers 2023–2026 and reveals a more recent and ongoing increase just East of Lukeville, AZ.

Segments

  • Seg 1 — W end · Cerro Colorado
  • Seg 2 — W of Lukeville
  • Seg 3 — Lukeville · Sonoyta
  • Seg 4 — E of Sonoyta
  • Seg 5 — E end · Ali Chuk
VIIRS/DNB · VNP46A1 Daily, cloud-masked, atmospherically
corrected nighttime radiance.
500 m nominal resolution.
Collection 2 (corrected for sensor
degradation post-2020 FSW event).
Study area
Five-segment extraction strip

The 48 km × 0.56 km strip runs along the ORPI southern boundary. Yellow tick marks indicate segment boundaries at 9.6 km intervals.

Google Earth imagery · Organ Pipe Cactus National Monument · Pima County, AZ · Segment boundaries at ~9.6 km intervals
Period 1 · 2020–2022
Border wall construction signal

Monthly medians of nightly radiance sum in nW/cm2/sr for each segment. The dominant features are construction lighting events in 2021.

VNP46A1 · OrganPipe_20 · 2020-01-01 → 2022-12-31 · Monthly medians shown as diamonds

Seg 1 & 2 — 2021 construction spike

Large blips in segments 1 and 2 are from construction lighting during the 2021 border wall build. Radiance returned to baseline after construction ceased.

Seg 3 — Lukeville · Sonoyta

Segment 3 is persistently bright due to the Lukeville/Sonoyta border crossing. A gradual decline may reflect the LED retrofit of municipal streetlighting — likely a spectral artefact in VIIRS rather than true dimming.

Seg 4 — Oct 2020 blip

A small spike in October 2020 may reflect other construction or temporary activity east of Sonoyta. It does not repeat and lies within the noise envelope of the baseline.

Seg 5 — quiescent

The eastern end near Ali Chuk shows no significant features across this period. Undulations in all segments are within the expected variability from cloud contamination and atmospheric variation.

Period 2 · 2023–2026
Ongoing increase in Segment 4

Post-construction baseline. All segments are stable except Segment 4, which shows a sustained and growing increase beginning in September 2025.

VNP46A1 · OrganPipe_23 · 2023-01-01 → 2026-05-29 · Monthly medians shown as diamonds

Segs 1, 2, 3, 5 — stable

No significant activity in 2023–2026. Segments 1, 2, and 5 remain at low baseline values. Segment 3 continues at its elevated level from the Lukeville/Sonoyta crossing.

Seg 4 — ~×3 increase from Sep 2025

A sustained, roughly threefold increase in radiance begins in September 2025 in Segment 4 (E of Sonoyta). The increase is ongoing through the end of the record. The source and cause are under investigation.

Southern Arizona Chapter · DarkSky International Data: NASA Earthdata · VNP46A1 C2
